section.title: Clean and Unclean
section.sectionRationale: Pharisees and scribes from Jerusalem confront Jesus over the tradition of the elders. He turns the charge around: their tradition actually nullifies the command of God. Then He redraws the whole map of purity, teaching that defilement is not a matter of hands and food but of the heart.
section.thesis: True defilement comes not from what enters a person from outside but from the sinful heart within, and human tradition that overrides God's word is a hollow, hypocritical worship that Jesus exposes and sweeps away.
section.frameNote: Three beats: the challenge over tradition and Jesus' counter-charge; the public saying about what truly defiles; and the private explanation that traces defilement to the heart.

units.label: The tradition of the elders
units.note: the word of God nullified by tradition

verses.text: Then some Pharisees and scribes came to Jesus from Jerusalem and asked,
“Why do Your disciples break the {{paradosis|tradition}} of the elders? They do not wash their hands before they eat.”
Jesus replied, “And why do you break the command of God for the sake of your tradition?
For God said, ‘Honor your father and mother’ and ‘Anyone who curses his father or mother must be put to death.’
But you say that if anyone says to his father or mother, ‘Whatever you would have received from me is a gift devoted to God,’
he need not honor his father or mother with it. Thus you nullify the word of God for the sake of your tradition.
You {{hypokrites|hypocrites}}! Isaiah prophesied correctly about you:
‘These people honor Me with their lips, but their hearts are far from Me.
They worship Me in vain; they teach as doctrine the precepts of men.’”
Jesus called the crowd to Him and said, “Listen and understand.
A man is not {{koinoo|defiled}} by what enters his mouth, but by what comes out of it.”
Then the disciples came to Him and said, “Are You aware that the Pharisees were offended when they heard this?”
But Jesus replied, “Every plant that My heavenly Father has not planted will be pulled up by its roots.
Disregard them! They are blind guides. If a blind man leads a blind man, both will fall into a pit.”

units.label: It is the heart that defiles
units.note: out of the heart come evil things

verses.text: Peter said to Him, “Explain this parable to us.”
“Do you still not understand?” Jesus asked.
“Do you not yet realize that whatever enters the mouth goes into the stomach and then is eliminated?
But the things that come out of the mouth come from the {{kardia|heart}}, and these things defile a man.
For out of the heart come evil thoughts, murder, adultery, sexual immorality, theft, false testimony, and slander.
These are what defile a man, but eating with unwashed hands does not defile him.”

paradosis.gloss: That which is handed down, a tradition. Here it is the oral tradition of the elders, the rulings added around the Law. Jesus does not condemn all handing down of truth, but exposes tradition that is raised above and against the written command of God.

hypokrites.gloss: An actor who speaks behind a mask, a pretender. Jesus names the leaders hypocrites because their worship is a performance of the lips while the heart is far from God, the very charge Isaiah leveled.

koinoo.gloss: To make common or unclean, to defile. The ceremonial law spoke of foods and contact that made one unclean; Jesus relocates true defilement from the outside of a person to the heart within, from ritual to moral.

kardia.gloss: The heart, the inner core of a person, the seat of thoughts, desires, and will. In Jesus' teaching the heart, not the hands or the stomach, is the source of what truly defiles, so cleansing must reach that deep.

basic.context: Religious leaders travel from Jerusalem to challenge Jesus: His disciples ignore the tradition of hand washing before meals. Jesus answers with a sharper question. Their traditions actually let people dodge God's own command to honor father and mother. He calls them hypocrites and quotes Isaiah. Then He tells the crowd that a person is not defiled by what goes into the mouth but by what comes out, and privately explains that evil comes from the heart.
basic.hermeneutics: The debate is not about hygiene but about authority: does human tradition or God's word rule? Notice the difference between the ceremonial food laws, which Jesus is beginning to fulfill and set aside, and the moral law of the heart, which stands. The key question is where uncleanness really comes from, and Jesus answers plainly: from within.
basic.originalLanguages: The leaders defend the paradosis, the tradition handed down. Jesus calls them hypokritai, actors wearing a mask, whose lips honor God while the heart is far away. He says nothing entering the mouth can koinoo, defile, a person; defilement rises from the kardia, the heart, the inner spring of thoughts and deeds.
basic.typology: The prophets longed for a day when God would deal with the heart, not just the surface. Jesus, by locating sin in the heart, points ahead to the new covenant promise of a new heart and a cleansing that ritual washing could never give.
basic.christ: Jesus speaks with an authority above the elders and even reframes the purity laws, showing Himself Lord of the Law. He is the one who diagnoses the true disease, the sinful heart, and who alone can cleanse it, making the unclean clean from the inside out.
basic.god: God is not fooled by outward religion. He sees the heart and is grieved by worship that is all lips and no love. He gave commands like honoring parents that tradition must never cancel, and He cares far more about mercy and truth than about ceremonial detail.
basic.crossReferences: Isaiah 29:13 lies behind these people honor Me with their lips. Jeremiah 31 and Ezekiel 36 promise a new heart. Mark 7 tells the same account and adds that Jesus thus declared all foods clean. Titus 1 speaks of the pure in heart.
basic.oneStory: Jesus exposes a religion of the surface and relocates the real problem to the heart. This sets the stage for the gospel: no washing of hands can cleanse a defiled heart, so the Son of God comes to give His people a new heart and to make the unclean clean by His own blood.

context.literary: This scene follows the feeding of the five thousand and the crowds pressing to touch Jesus, and it sharpens the conflict with the leaders that has been building since chapter 12. It pairs a public controversy with a private explanation to the disciples, a pattern Matthew uses often, and it prepares for the very next scene where Jesus, in Gentile territory, heals a Canaanite woman's daughter.
context.historical: The tradition of the elders was a growing body of oral rulings meant to build a hedge around the written Law. Ritual hand washing before meals was one such rule, not commanded in the Torah for ordinary people. The Corban vow, declaring property devoted to God, could be abused to withhold support from aging parents, exactly the abuse Jesus names.
context.canonical: Jesus stands in the line of the prophets who denounced hollow worship, quoting Isaiah 29:13. His teaching that defilement is of the heart anticipates the new covenant promises of Jeremiah 31 and Ezekiel 36, and it opens the door, developed in Acts 10 and the letters, to setting aside the ceremonial food laws while upholding the moral law.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show Jesus overturning a religion of external tradition, defending the authority of God's word against human additions, and teaching that true purity and defilement are matters of the heart.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The rebuke of the Pharisees describes a specific abuse, yet it prescribes an abiding warning against elevating tradition over Scripture and mistaking outward observance for real holiness. Jesus' teaching that the heart defiles is a permanent truth about human sin.

debates.question: Is Jesus abolishing the ceremonial food laws here, or only correcting their misuse?

views.label: He sets aside the ceremonial food laws
views.case: By saying nothing entering the mouth defiles, Jesus relativizes the clean and unclean food distinctions; Mark 7:19 makes this explicit, thus He declared all foods clean, and Acts 10 confirms the shift.

views.label: He is chiefly correcting the tradition of the elders
views.case: The immediate target is hand washing, a human tradition, not the Torah itself; Jesus upholds the moral command to honor parents, so the point is the priority of God's word over added rules.

debates.bearing: Both agree that the moral law of the heart abides; the fuller New Testament witness shows Jesus fulfilling the ceremonial law so that food no longer defines God's people, while its deeper aim, a clean heart, is realized in Him.

study.originalLanguages: The leaders appeal to paradosis, the tradition handed down from the elders, and set it over the command of God. Jesus answers with Isaiah's word and calls them hypokritai, masked actors whose lips move while the heart stays far from God. He then dismantles their whole scheme of purity: nothing that enters the mouth can koinoo, make common or unclean, a person, because it passes through the body and is gone. What defiles rises from the kardia, the heart, the deep spring from which come evil thoughts, murder, adultery, immorality, theft, false witness, and slander. Cleansing, then, must reach the heart, which is exactly what Jesus came to do.

typology.type: The ritual washings and purity laws
typology.fulfillment: Shadows of a deeper cleansing; Jesus fulfills them by dealing with the defiled heart, so that purity is now of the inner person, Hebrews 9 and 10.
Fulfilled in the leaders who oppose Jesus, and answered in the true worshipers who worship in spirit and truth, John 4.
Jesus' diagnosis of the heart points to the new covenant gift of a cleansed and renewed heart, Jeremiah 31, Ezekiel 36.

study.christ: Jesus confronts the guardians of tradition with an authority higher than theirs, judging the elders by the word of God and reframing the ancient purity laws. He is the Lord of the Law, free to declare where true defilement lies. More than a teacher, He is the great physician of the heart: He names the disease that no ritual can touch, the corruption within, and He alone provides the cure. By His death He cleanses the conscience, and by His Spirit He gives a new heart, so that the unclean are made clean not by washing the outside but by being remade within.
study.god: The God revealed here searches the heart and will not accept a worship of lips without love. He is grieved by religion that honors Him in words while the heart is distant, and He is jealous for His own commands, so that no human tradition may cancel them, not even under a religious pretext like a gift devoted to God. He cares that children honor their parents, that mercy is not smothered by rules, and above all that His people be clean where it counts, in the heart.

crossReferences.note: These people honor Me with their lips, the prophecy Jesus applies to the leaders.
The parallel account, which adds that Jesus thus declared all foods clean.
The new covenant promise to write God's law on the heart.
God will sprinkle clean water, give a new heart, and put His Spirit within.

study.oneStory: The conflict over clean and unclean drives to the deepest problem in the human story: the heart is defiled, and no washing of hands can reach it. Jesus exposes a religion of the surface and relocates sin to its true source, then presents Himself as the one with authority over the Law and power over the heart. The story runs on toward the cross, where He deals with defilement once for all, and toward the new covenant, where God gives His people the clean heart the prophets promised.

quiz.q: What was the real problem with the Pharisees' tradition of hand washing?

options.text: They elevated human tradition above God's command, even using it to sidestep His word.
options.note: Verses 3-6 show tradition being used to nullify the command to honor parents.

options.text: They added extra rules that were not in the Law of Moses.
options.note: True, the washing was a human addition, but the deeper fault was setting tradition against God's own command.

options.text: They were too concerned about physical cleanliness and hygiene.
options.note: The issue is not hygiene at all but authority and the heart; this misplaces the emphasis.

options.text: They failed to wash their hands often enough themselves.
options.note: Jesus is not endorsing the washing rule; the charge is against the tradition, not their neglect of it.

quiz.q: According to Jesus, what truly defiles a person?

options.text: The evil that comes out of the heart, such as evil thoughts, malice, and deceit.
options.note: Verses 18-19 trace defilement to the heart within.

options.text: The sinful words and actions a person produces.
options.note: True, these defile, but Jesus traces them back to their source, the heart, which this answer omits.

options.text: Certain unclean foods that enter the mouth.
options.note: Jesus explicitly denies this; what enters the mouth does not defile the person.

options.text: Contact with ceremonially unclean people or objects.
options.note: Jesus relocates defilement from external contact to the inner heart.

quiz.q: How does Jesus describe the worship of the leaders, quoting Isaiah?

options.text: They honor God with their lips while their hearts are far from Him.
options.note: Verses 8-9 quote Isaiah 29:13 on hollow, hypocritical worship.

options.text: They worship sincerely but follow the wrong traditions.
options.note: Partly right about the traditions, but Jesus calls the worship itself vain and the heart distant, not sincere.

options.text: They worship God correctly but lack enough zeal.
options.note: The problem is not too little zeal but a heart far from God behind the outward show.

options.text: They no longer perform any worship at all.
options.note: They worship diligently, but in vain; the issue is the heart, not the absence of worship.

quiz.q: What does this passage reveal about the kind of cleansing people need?

options.text: A cleansing of the heart, which no external ritual can accomplish.
options.note: Since defilement rises from the heart, cleansing must reach that deep, pointing to the new covenant work of Christ.

options.text: A more careful keeping of the outward purity laws.
options.note: This gets the seriousness of purity but looks in the wrong place; the heart, not the outside, is the issue.

options.text: A cleansing that comes simply by human effort and self-discipline.
options.note: The heart's corruption runs too deep for self-reform; it needs the renewing work Jesus brings.

options.text: No cleansing at all, since the heart cannot be changed.
options.note: Scripture promises a new heart; Jesus comes precisely to cleanse and renew it.

questions: Jesus warned against letting tradition override God's word. Where might a comfortable custom or assumption be quietly displacing what Scripture actually says in your life?
The leaders honored God with their lips while their hearts were far from Him. What would it look like for your worship this week to close that gap between words and heart?
Jesus traced sin to the heart, not the hands. When you are tempted to manage sin by managing appearances, how does His diagnosis change the way you pray?
The Corban loophole let people look devout while neglecting their parents. Is there a place where religious language is masking a duty of love you are avoiding?
Only a new heart can make the unclean clean. How does knowing that Jesus cleanses from the inside out free you from mere outward striving?

translationNotes.note: Jesus cites Exodus 20:12 and Deuteronomy 5:16 (honor your father and mother) together with Exodus 21:17 and Leviticus 20:9; the two quotations are joined here with the word and for a clean reading.
The gift devoted to God reflects the Corban vow; some manuscripts read his father or mother, others his father only. The sense is unchanged: the vow was used to withhold support owed to parents.
The quotation is Isaiah 29:13; it is presented here as prose for a clean, dash-free reading rather than set as poetry.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Matthew 15:1-20. Verse text assembled by concatenating segment values in order; heading, line-break, poem, and note markers were skipped. No em or en dashes appear. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
